mnemonics.title: CAPACITY Planning Checklist
mnemonics.content:
C - Cost analysis (CapEx + OpEx)
A - Analyze current utilization
P - Predict future demands
A - Assess bottleneck locations
C - Calculate required resources
I - Identify scaling strategy (vertical/horizontal)
T - Test before deployment
Y - Yield monitoring post-implementation

mnemonics.title: Effects of Capacity Increase
mnemonics.content:
P - Performance improvement
E - Expenses increase (power, cooling)
R - Returns diminish (beyond knee)
F - Fault tolerance improves (horizontal)
O - Overhead grows (complexity)
R - Resources need management
M - Migration of bottlenecks occurs
A - Availability increases
N - Network demands grow
C - Complexity increases
E - Economies of scale apply

acronyms.usage: Includes CapEx + OpEx over the lifecycle of IT capacity additions
Measures the financial benefit of capacity increases against costs
Defines performance targets that capacity planning must meet
Metrics that capacity planning aims to maintain or improve

visualTips.visualization: Draw an S-curve: flat at low capacity (underutilized), steep rise (optimal), then flattening (diminishing returns). The 'knee' is the inflection point.
Imagine a water pipe system: widening one pipe just moves the constraint to the next narrow pipe downstream.
Compare two lines: one for benefits (curve flattening), one for costs (linear or step). The gap between them is the ROI zone.

analogies.analogy: Adding more lanes to a highway: first few lanes dramatically reduce traffic, but adding more lanes beyond 8-10 has minimal impact as exits become bottlenecks.
A restaurant kitchen: upgrading the stove won't help if the prep station or waitstaff can't keep up—constraints shift between stations.
A parking lot at 75% capacity leaves spaces for visitors while avoiding lot overflow—just right!
Instead of building a bigger truck, use multiple delivery vans—more reliable if one breaks down, and easier to expand.
